So I added one.
The documentation to PySequence_Check states, that any object with a __getitem__ method may be a sequence, but this is not a definite test.
So I think, each library, that uses this function has to handle the corner cases by itself.
But it is definitely worth discussing, to add more checks to PySequence_Check.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2387131993/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27382","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27382/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27382/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27382/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27382","id":2522665845,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86WXNN1","number":27382,"title":"BUG: numpy.array does not respect `__iter__ = None`","user":{"login":"ruema","id":1430751,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE0MzA3NTE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1430751?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ruema","html_url":"https://github.com/ruema","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ruema/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ruema/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ruema/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ruema/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ruema/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ruema/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ruema/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ruema/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ruema/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":6359214,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MzU5MjE0","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/00%20-%20Bug","name":"00 - Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-09-12T15:03:46Z","updated_at":"2024-09-12T15:03:46Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

Since python3 `__iter__ = None` means, this object is not iterable.
Creating an array of `dtype=object` with such objects leads to a `TypeError`

I would expect, that numpy doesn't investigate these objects any deeper, and simply puts them into the created array,


### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np

class SomeClass:
    def __init__(self, size):
        self.size = size

    __iter__ = None  # this object is not iterable

    def __getitem__(self, index):
        if index < self.size:
            return index ** 2
        raise IndexError(index)

    def __len__(self):
        return self.size

array = np.array([SomeClass(5), SomeClass(7)], dtype=object)
```


### Error message:

```shell
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "./iter.py", line 17, in <module>
    array = np.array([SomeClass(5), SomeClass(7)], dtype=object)
TypeError: Could not convert object to sequence
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

2.0.2
3.9.16 (main, Mar  8 2023, 14:00:05) 
[GCC 11.2.0]

### Runtime Environment:

_No response_

### Context for the issue:

to_string"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":9,"created_at":"2017-12-19T15:39:37Z","updated_at":"2024-09-18T11:23:24Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"#### Code Sample, a copy-pastable example if possible

```
class foo(dict):
    def __init__(self, iterable=None, **kwargs):
        if iterable is None:
            iterable = ()
        super(foo, self).__init__(iterable)
        self.update(kwargs)
    def __repr__(self):
        return ",".join(self.keys())
    def __str__(self):
        return ",".join(self.keys())

f = foo({'alpha' : 'b',
    'beta' : 'c'})

import pandas as pd
pd.DataFrame(data=[['A', 1, f]], columns=['D', 'F', 'G'])

```
#### Problem description

For a given series with a custom object, I want to control the content when displayed via print or displaying on ipython notebooks.  The object ```foo``` is a simple class that have the ```__str__``` and ```__repr__``` overwritten, but still displays the object's dictionary content, not the view I want to show the end users.  How do I control that?



#### Expected Output

```alpha,beta```

what I get is:

```{'alpha': 'b', 'beta': 'c'}```

#### Output of ``pd.show_versions()``

```python
from collections import namedtuple
import pandas as pd

Point = namedtuple("Point", "x,y")
series = pd.Series({'pos': Point(7,3)})
print(series)
# pos    (7, 3)
# dtype: object


class Point(namedtuple("Point", "x,y")):
    __next__ = None

series = pd.Series({'pos': Point(7,3)})
print(series)
# pos    Point(x=7, y=3)
# dtype: object
```
I wonder, whether pandas pretty printing is helpful for anything but lists and tuples.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/comments/2359414321/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}
